chunk_summary: Lactogenesis progresses through three stages, with milk production influenced by hormonal changes, feeding practices, and maternal factors. Early milk volumes are small, and delayed lactogenesis requires structured interventions to optimize breastfeeding outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the three stages of lactogenesis and their physiological triggers.
Identify factors that can delay lactogenesis stage 2 and their clinical implications.
Learn strategies to support milk production, including frequent milk removal and structured expression plans.
key_facts: Lactogenesis stage 1 begins during pregnancy with colostrum production.
Lactogenesis stage 2, marked by copious milk production, typically starts 48–72 hours postpartum.
Delayed lactogenesis stage 2 can occur after cesarean birth, postpartum hemorrhage, maternal obesity, or diabetes.
Frequent and effective milk removal is critical to maintaining milk supply.
Formula supplementation without concurrent expression can delay lactogenesis stage 2.
reference_quote: Lactogenesis stage 2 is the onset of copious milk production, typically around 48–72 hours postpartum, triggered by placental delivery and falling progesterone in the setting of continued suckling. (Source: NCBI Bookshelf)
